speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed Swenson tells about a foolish miner from the park country, about early mail services to the settlement, about the first alfalfa grown there, and about an accident that could have killed his father and its funny aftermath.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you hear about the miner? What did you know about him from the mine?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, he was a very modest, quiet man, and stayed by himself pretty much. He came to our place a few times and talked about his mining and his work down there. But he was not very clear in his ideas about things, so we didn't get very much of it. But he did do some drilling and blasting. He would sink a shaft instead of tunneling, and drill down a little ways, and put in a small charge of dynamite, and blast that loose, and then clean that out, and then drill down a little further. He never did go to any depth at all. If he didn't find gold where he was drilling, he'd shift over to some other place, and start another little shaft there, and sort of work around. But he finally gave it up. He began to get older, and he gave it up. He'd come around and wish it, and talk about his place down there. He didn't do any plowing or clearing of land, because his house was part way down the canyon. He built his house on a little spot of Lavinland, and cleared the little for a few fruit trees and a garden, and that was about it. And in later years we didn't hear more about him, because we moved away from there. So I really don't know what happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story that they told about him almost having an accident one time?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: About him almost having an accident. At one time he was working in his mine, and was down a few feet, and he didn't have much money to spend for dynamite and juice, so he cut his fuse pretty short. He set a charge and he climbed out, but he fell back in again, and almost didn't make it. He just cut out the time to be saved on that. And that kind of frightened him so that he didn't go very deep with his work, and he finally quit it all together. And you also said that he was religious, he had some ideas about the... Yes, he claimed that he would have revelations from God at times about how to live and how to do. One day a strong wind came, and trees bent with the wind, and he said that God revealed to him there that we should be humbled and bow our heads in reverence to God.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Now, at one time he came to my brother's place, and my brother asked him to stay overnight. He stayed into the evening, so he decided to stay overnight. Then he told my brother that he was going to die that night. God had revealed to him that he was going to die, so he said goodbye to my brother before he went to bed. Next morning my brother looked into the bedroom and he was sleeping soundly, so my brother made a noise to wake him up, and then he rushed over to the bed just the way he was going to carry him out of there. And Nelson said good morning, and my brother said he was surprised, he said I'm surprised that he was going to die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said what?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He said I'm surprised that you're still living because you said that God was going to take him that night. So the man smiled sheepishly and said that he was going to go that time.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, that's a good story. So I guess he didn't take him too seriously. No, of course not. Often he had nice remarks, you know, that he didn't have any meaning. He was harmless. He had, you might say, friendship with old people because he meant well and he never argued on a point to cause any hard feelings.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So whenever he came to our place we'd ask him to stay a while and have dinner with us and talk a while and he seemed to enjoy that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But he didn't mention to conversation about anything except just what he was doing and how he felt.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I wanted to ask you too about how you mentioned this morning that the alfalfa started getting grown in the country.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: How the alfalfa started getting grown. Yeah, at first the farmers at the park didn't think alfalfa would grow there because the season was shorter. They knew that alfalfa did well down in the Lewiston Valley. So one day one of my neighbors brought home a handful of seed and he planted a little grow in the garden in the spring and the alfalfa did very well. People began to buy a seed and seed the land down to alfalfa for hay instead of oats. And after that they had alfalfa on all the farms there. So that was the beginning of it, just this little, this little, this little seed. And I can remember he was so proud he was the first man that had alfalfa on his farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was he?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Steiner Hellerud. He was an uncle to Helmer and Steiner over here.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Another thing that you mentioned to me once before was about the beginnings of the post office in Park, how that came about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you tell me that?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, at first we got our mail over on Texas Strait at a place called Anderson Post Office. There was a little grocery store there. And eventually then after the valley began to get settled, the people wanted a way to get the mail a little closer. So they called a meeting in the schoolhouse and discussed this matter. And then they applied to the government to get a post office. And they submitted the name Park Post Office because the valley looked so much like a park. And this was accepted and a park post office was established then. So then twice a week somebody would volunteer to go over to Anderson to pick up the mail and bring it over to Park Valley. And one of the neighbors was appointed to distribute the mail. So those days when the mail was brought in we would go over there and get our mail. At that time there was no parcel post system so it was just a first class mail and a few newspapers and things like that. So the mail could be brought on one horse.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: This man then became the postmaster.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: A man named Andrew Hellerud was the postmaster.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And this carried on until eventually a little store was built up there and the post office was moved over to there. So they had the mail there and then the route was changed over to Southwick Idaho. So our address was to Southwick Idaho and the mail was brought and the rural route was established. So part of that route between Park and Southwick was for a little post office established at the farm sites on Cedar Creek. And the part mail was brought over that far and then a rider would go over to that place to get the mail and bring it to Park. And Boxers would put up along the route so that he would deliver the mail on that end of the route between there and Park post office. The name of that little place was Linden Post Office.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And the mail was opened there and the part mail was put in a separate mail bag to be sent to Park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was that switch made about do you know from?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That could be about, well about 1907 or along in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was much trading ever done with Kendrick from Park?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, the people living in that part of the valley closer to Kendrick would go there to do their shopping but most of it went to Park.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I have one more thing to ask you about and that is, I'd like you to tell me a little about why you are so interested in history, how you happen to remember so much of the old days.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I was the youngest boy in our family and I stayed with my parents as I grew up and spent so much more time at Park and in the district surrounding there so that I can remember how the environment changed from time to time. And I also think back to those times so that it kept fresh in my memory.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Until we moved away in the fall of 1910. After there, that time there has been money changes made so that the valley really doesn't look like it was at first there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But you have spent quite a bit of time thinking about it in later years.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: After my wife died in the year 1955, I was more lonesome and when I had idle time I would begin to think back more about the early days up to that time and that is how I can remember it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that before you left Park that these changes were in the environment, were small changes? Do you feel that when you were in Park up until 1910, these changes that you saw were small changes?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, the things didn't change very much during those years. The original homesteaders were almost all there and the work carried on didn't change much from year to year. But after the pilot company had taken the timber out and the times began to get better than some of the old timers moved to other places. They sold their land because other people came that wanted to settle in there and rocking more machinery and livestock. And some of the old timers had died so the neighborhood began to change from year to year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember the names of the first homesteading families that were in Park when you arrived? Yes, you mean the ones that were living there when we came?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There were two brothers, Andrew Hellerud and Steiner Hellerud. And their sister married my oldest brother and he came west just about a year after the Helleruds settled there. Then the other homesteaders there at that time was Ole Torgeson and Mr. Oelleberg and family.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And Pete Enger, he was a bachelor or a single man at that time, and Henry Jurgensen, a bachelor,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: andMr. Goldstrom, I do not remember his given name, Mr. Goldstrom and Mr. Gurgensen were the first two men that ever came to Park. They were miners for the time being. If you did, you told me about them.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I would also like to know if you can remember who the families were that came soon after you came.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: After we came there, that would be Mr. and Mrs. Oscar Bohn and family.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Mr. Requa, I cannot remember his first name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his last name?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Requa. R -E -Q -U -A.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you spell Bohn?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: B -O -H -N. Mr. and Mrs. Lars Bohn.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Before that, they farmed out that Tennessee, that is. And Tom Elingson, a bachelor,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: and Mr. and Mrs.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I do not remember their first name, but their last name was Torgeson also.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And Edward Ruberg, Eling Guningsrud was one of the oldtimers, and I believe that was all

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you be willing to make up, to draw up a list of these names for me when you get back down to Southern Idaho? So we can have the spellings right?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That would be very good. If you send that to Viola, well, I'll get it from her.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Send it up here and I'll do it again.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Okay, there's just one more question that I think I have, and that is, is the reason that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did other families besides your fathers work out in the Genesee Valley?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, yeah, they all went to go out to get a grub stake.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is the reason that they worked there because they spoke Norwegian in Genesee Valley, too?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, that is Norwegian settlement.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Were there many people that were related to people from there, or who knew them from the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Older times?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I can't say about that. You mean as a neighborhood there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, like families that were related from one to the other. No, I can't remember that there were because I never knew of any two families that had the same name. So they must have been paper from other parts of the east or west.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did other people from Park who came to Park come as you did because they had relatives in Park?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Not so many. The later settlers that came in there had different names, and were from different countries. And just put some posts in the ground and put a pole roof on and shakes. The sides were still open there, just to kind of keep the snow off the wood, you know?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So he and my older brothers were going to kind of remodel it. In doing that, one of them had taken off a brace that supported the framework there. Father was underneath the roof there, just about in the center of the shed, along a big post there. And the shed started waving and fell. And Father fell. It so happened he fell right alongside of this post. That saved his, perhaps, his life. And my older brothers, right away, they tore open the roof to get Father out. Well, a neighbor named Ole Sunby was breaking up the first piece of land that was broke on that place, about an acre of ground. He was breaking there close by. And he saw, looked back and saw the woodshed laying flat on the ground, and they were taking Father out. He came running, but then he got there. Father was on his feet and wasn't injured. So he and Mr. Sunby was always chasing each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Father said, what are you coming for now?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He said, you were too late to do anything. Father had to pair a new shoe, so. And Olesaid, I didn't come to help you. He said, I thought maybe I could get your shoes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a lot of sympathy, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I suppose they joke with each other a lot in the old days. Yeah, the old days. They sure did also know each other pretty well and know their habits and their ways of doing them, and know that they could take a joke, but they sure lay at home sometimes.
